story.title: Morning in Maplewood
story.description: Leo is eleven years old and lives in a quiet street in Maplewood. Follow his everyday life as small moments — a basketball tryout, a neighbour's kindness, a difficult day — teach him something new about himself.
story.author: ReadWell

vocabulary.definition: a set of actions that you do regularly, always in the same order
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo's morning routine starts when his alarm rings at seven o'clock.

vocabulary.definition: a device that makes a loud noise at a set time to wake you up
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo's alarm rings every morning at exactly seven o'clock.

vocabulary.definition: to get something ready before you need it
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo prepares his school bag every morning by checking his timetable first.

vocabulary.definition: a food made from grains, usually eaten with milk for breakfast
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo eats the same cereal every morning because he likes the taste.

vocabulary.definition: a person who lives very close to you, usually on the same street
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Their neighbour Mrs Park waves at them every morning from her garden.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use the simple present tense to talk about habits, routines, and things that are always true. For he/she/it, we add -s or -es to the verb. We use do/does to make questions and negatives.
examples: Leo eats cereal for breakfast every morning.
Mrs Park waters her plants before eight o'clock.
Leo's father does not wake him up — the alarm does.
Does Mia like mornings? No, she doesn't.

content.text: Leo wakes up every weekday at seven o'clock. His alarm rings once, and he turns it off immediately. He lies still for one moment, then gets up. This is his routine. He always does the same things in the same order: he puts on his glasses, washes his face, and walks downstairs. His father is already in the kitchen when Leo arrives.

Leo eats cereal for breakfast. He always has the same kind. He pours the milk carefully and reads the back of the box while he eats. His younger sister Mia comes downstairs at twenty past seven. She sits across from him and stares at her toast without speaking. Mia does not like mornings. She never says good morning before eight o'clock.

After breakfast, Leo prepares his bag for school. He checks his timetable on the fridge door. Today is Wednesday, so he needs his PE kit and science folder. He puts them in his bag, then checks again. His father says Leo is careful to the point of being slow. Leo says there is no such thing as too careful. His father laughs.

At ten past eight, Leo and Mia leave the house. Their neighbour Mrs Park is always in her front garden at this time. She waters her plants every morning, even when it rains. She waves at Leo and Mia as they walk past. "Morning, Leo! Morning, Mia!" she calls. "Morning, Mrs Park!" they call back. Leo likes this small part of the walk.

They arrive at school at twenty-five past eight. Leo's best friend Daniel is waiting at the gate because he lives around the corner. "You look tired," Daniel says. "I'm not tired," Leo says. "I just wake up slowly." Daniel grins. "But I look awake." The bell rings at half past eight. Leo and Daniel walk in together. The day begins. For Leo, it always begins the same way — and he likes it.

comprehension.question: What does Leo do FIRST after his alarm rings?
options: He goes to the bathroom.
He puts on his glasses.
He turns off the alarm and lies still for a moment.
He goes downstairs to the kitchen.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He turns off the alarm and lies still for a moment.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 says: 'His alarm rings once, and he turns it off immediately. He lies still for one moment — just one — then gets up.' This happens before he puts on his glasses or goes to the bathroom.

comprehension.question: Why does Leo check the timetable before preparing his bag?
options: He forgets what day it is every morning.
He wants to know which books and equipment he needs for that day.
His father asks him to check it.
He reads it for fun, like the cereal box.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He wants to know which books and equipment he needs for that day.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 says Leo checks his timetable and sees it is Wednesday, so he knows he needs his PE kit and science folder. He prepares his bag based on what the timetable tells him.

comprehension.question: What does 'Mia does not like mornings' suggest about Mia's behaviour?
options: Mia refuses to go to school.
Mia is slow, quiet, and unfriendly in the morning.
Mia prefers to sleep until lunchtime.
Mia argues with Leo every morning.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Mia is slow, quiet, and unfriendly in the morning.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 2 tells us Mia 'stares at her toast without speaking' and 'never says good morning before eight o'clock.' These details show she is quiet and not cheerful in the mornings, even if the story does not say she is 'unfriendly' directly.

comprehension.question: What does the word 'routine' mean in paragraph 1?
options: A type of alarm clock
A set of actions done regularly in the same order
A path that Leo walks to school
The time that school starts
comprehension.correctAnswer: A set of actions done regularly in the same order
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 1, after Leo says 'This is his routine', the story describes exactly what he does every morning in the same order: glasses, bathroom, face, downstairs. This shows that a routine is a regular set of actions done in the same order.

vocabulary.definition: a test or short performance to see if someone is good enough to join a team or group
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo arrived early for the basketball tryout because he was very nervous.

vocabulary.definition: to give all your attention to one thing
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo tried to concentrate on the ball and not think about the other students watching him.

vocabulary.definition: to become better at something, or to make something better
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Leo practised every evening because he wanted to improve his shooting.

vocabulary.definition: feeling sure about your own abilities
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Some of the other students at the tryout looked very confident.

vocabulary.definition: to say or do something that gives someone more confidence
vocabulary.exampleSentence: Daniel always encourages Leo when he feels like giving up.

grammarFocus.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about actions that are happening right now or around now. The form is: am/is/are + verb-ing. We also use it for temporary situations that are not permanent habits.
examples: Leo is standing outside the gym and his heart is beating fast.
Two boys are shooting hoops at the far end of the court.
Coach Williams is writing something on his clipboard.
"What are you doing?" "I'm concentrating — please be quiet."

content.text: Leo is standing outside the gym with other students. He is wearing new trainers and holding a basketball in his hands. His heart is beating fast. Today is the basketball tryout, and Leo has practised for weeks. Right now he is not feeling confident. Two boys are taller. A girl near the wall is dribbling quickly. Leo is watching and wondering if he belongs.

Inside the gym, Coach Williams is standing in the centre of the court with a clipboard and whistle. He is not smiling, but he does not look unfriendly. He calls each student one by one. When he calls Leo's name, Leo walks onto the court. The gym is bright and loud. "Show me your dribble," says Coach Williams. Leo bounces the ball. He is concentrating so hard that his forehead hurts.

Leo runs with the ball towards the basket. He is watching the floor, the ring, and his hands. He jumps and shoots. The ball hits the rim and bounces off. "Again," says Coach Williams. Leo shoots again. This time, the ball goes in. "Not bad," says Coach Williams. "Keep going." Leo finds it easier to concentrate when he stops thinking about the other students. He is just playing. For a few minutes, he is only running and shooting and improving.

grammarAnnotations.targetText: is only running and shooting and improving

content.text: After the tryout, Leo is sitting on the bench by the gym door. He is breathing hard and drinking from his water bottle. Coach Williams is still writing. Daniel calls through the door: "How did it go?" Leo shrugs. "He's writing things down," Leo says. "That's good," Daniel says. "He only writes things down about people who are interesting." Leo does not know if that is true, but Daniel always knows how to encourage him.

The results are going on the board tomorrow morning. Tonight, Leo is sitting at the dinner table with his father and Mia. He is eating slowly and not saying much. "How was school?" his father asks. "Fine," Leo says. His father looks at him. "And the tryout?" Leo thinks for a moment. "I think I improved," he says. "I wasn't the best, but I was better than I expected." His father nods. "That's all you can ask." Mia looks up. "Did you get in?" she asks. "Tomorrow," Leo says.

comprehension.question: Why does Leo feel unconfident when he is waiting outside the gym?
options: He forgot to bring his basketball shoes.
He sees that some other students look more skilled and bigger than him.
He has never played basketball before.
Coach Williams tells him he is too short.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He sees that some other students look more skilled and bigger than him.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 1 says 'Two of the boys are taller than him' and describes a girl dribbling 'quickly without even looking at it.' These details explain why Leo is not feeling confident.

comprehension.question: What is Leo doing when Coach Williams says 'Not bad'?
options: He is running with the ball for the first time.
He has just made a successful shot after missing the first one.
He is talking to Daniel through the gym door.
He is waiting on the bench after the tryout.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He has just made a successful shot after missing the first one.
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 3 shows the sequence: Leo shoots, the ball misses ('hits the rim and bounces off'), he shoots again, and this time 'the ball goes in.' It is after this successful shot that Coach Williams says 'Not bad.'

comprehension.question: What does 'concentrate' mean in paragraph 2?
options: To run as fast as possible
To give all your attention to one thing
To feel nervous before a competition
To follow the coach's instructions carefully
comprehension.correctAnswer: To give all your attention to one thing
comprehension.explanation: Paragraph 2 says Leo 'is concentrating so hard that his forehead hurts.' He is trying to focus completely on what he is doing — dribbling the ball — which shows that 'concentrate' means to direct all your attention to one thing.

comprehension.question: How does Leo feel about his performance at the end of the story?
options: He is certain he will make the team.
He is disappointed because he made mistakes.
He feels he improved, even if he was not the best.
He thinks the tryout was unfair.
comprehension.correctAnswer: He feels he improved, even if he was not the best.
comprehension.explanation: In paragraph 5, Leo says directly: 'I wasn't the best, but I was better than I expected.' This is his honest assessment of how he did — not perfect, but an improvement.
